title: The cold backup
prompt: Real work arrives as archives. backup.tar.gz is a compressed tarball: tar -tzf lists what's inside without extracting; tar -xzf extracts it. Unpack it, read what you find — the key file tells you where the real vault is. (Spoiler: the vault is hidden, and you know how to see hidden things.)

details.restate: Extract backup.tar.gz (a backup/ folder comes out of it), read backup/KEY.txt, and follow what it says to the hidden vault file. Submit the vault's flag. Both steps are checked: the archive must actually be extracted.

io.because: -t lists the contents without touching your disk
-x extracts, -z handles the gzip, -f names the file

terms.meaning: many files packed into one (.tar), usually gzip-compressed (.tar.gz) — THE unit of moving data around Unix

help.plain: List it, extract it, read the key, then use a trick from three missions ago.
help.analogy: A shipping crate with a packing slip inside — and the slip says the valuables shipped separately.
help.mnemonic: tar -tzf to peek, tar -xzf to open. eXtract, gZip, File.
help.nudge: After extraction, KEY.txt names a path you can't see with plain ls. Why not — and which flag fixes that?
